source_paper_title: On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Luner_2025, title={On Averaging and Extrapolation for Gradient Descent}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2024.0449}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2024.0449}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Luner, Alan and Grimmer, Benjamin}, year={2025}, month=jul }
context:
Section 3.2.1 (Discussion of Conditions (C1) and (C2)), page 12, Conjecture 3.3.

Numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h = (h_0, \dots, h_{N-1}) > 0\). For any \(L, D > 0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr) - \inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \Bigl\|\nabla f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr)\Bigr\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.\]
title: Prove averaging helps gradient descent exactly when last-iterate Huber worst case fails
background_latex:
Let \(f:\mathbb{R}^m\to\mathbb{R}\) be convex and \(L\)-smooth (i.e., \(\nabla f\) is \(L\)-Lipschitz in the Euclidean norm). Assume a minimizer \(x^\star\) exists and the initial point satisfies \(\|x_0-x^\star\|\le D\).

Fix an integer \(N\ge 1\) and a predetermined stepsize sequence \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})\) with \(h_k>0\). Gradient descent generates iterates
\[x_{k+1}=x_k-\frac{h_k}{L}\nabla f(x_k),\qquad k=0,\dots,N-1.\]

An averaging scheme is a weight vector \(\sigma=(\sigma_0,\dots,\sigma_N)\) in the simplex \(\Delta_{N+1}=\{\sigma\in\mathbb{R}_+^{N+1}:\sum_{j=0}^N\sigma_j=1\}\). The reported point is
\[x_\sigma:=\sum_{j=0}^N\sigma_j x_j.\]
Call \(\sigma\) nondegenerate if \(x_\sigma\ne x_N\) (equivalently, \(\sigma\ne (0,\dots,0,1)\)).

Define the worst-case final-iterate objective-gap and gradient-norm values over the class
\(\mathcal{F}_{L,D}:=\{(x_0,f): f\text{ convex, }L\text{-smooth, and }\|x_0-x^\star\|\le D\}\) by
\[W_f(h):=\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_N)-\inf f\bigr),\qquad W_g(h):=\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_N)\|.\]
A particular candidate “Huber-attained” characterization is
\[(\mathrm{C1})\quad W_f(h)=\frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+2},\qquad (\mathrm{C2})\quad W_g(h)=\frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+1},\]
which holds for several stepsize families studied in the paper, and is attained by a one-dimensional Huber-type convex \(L\)-smooth function.

The averaging worst-case problems are
\[\inf_{\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}}\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_\sigma)-\inf f\bigr),\qquad \inf_{\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}}\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\|.\]
The paper proves that if (C1) holds then the first infimum is uniquely minimized at \(\sigma=(0,\dots,0,1)\), and similarly if (C2) holds then the second infimum is uniquely minimized at \(\sigma=(0,\dots,0,1)\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the following converse statements hold for gradient descent with arbitrary fixed stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\).

1) Objective-gap converse: If
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_N)-\inf f\bigr)\ne \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+2},\]
then there exists a nondegenerate \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_\sigma)-\inf f\bigr)<\frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+2}.
\]

2) Gradient-norm converse: If
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_N)\|\ne \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+1},\]
then there exists a nondegenerate \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\|<\frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+1}.
\]
In other words, characterize whether failure of the Huber-attained last-iterate worst-case forms (C1) and (C2) is equivalent to the existence of an averaging scheme that strictly improves the corresponding worst-case guarantee over reporting \(x_N\).

Determine whether the following converse statements hold for gradient descent with arbitrary fixed stepsizes \(h=(h_0,\dots,h_{N-1})>0\).

1) Objective-gap converse: If
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_N)-\inf f\bigr)\ne \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+2},\]
then there exists a nondegenerate \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\bigl(f(x_\sigma)-\inf f\bigr)<\frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+2}.
\]

2) Gradient-norm converse: If
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_N)\|\ne \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+1},\]
then there exists a nondegenerate \(\sigma\in\Delta_{N+1}\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}}\|\nabla f(x_\sigma)\|<\frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1}h_i+1}.
\]
In other words, characterize whether failure of the Huber-attained last-iterate worst-case forms (C1) and (C2) is equivalent to the existence of an averaging scheme that strictly improves the corresponding worst-case guarantee over reporting \(x_N\).
location_in_paper: Section 3.2.1 (Discussion of Conditions (C1) and (C2)), page 12, Conjecture 3.3.
open_problem_quote:
Numerical results suggest that the converses of Theorem 3.1 and Theorem 3.2 are true, i.e., (C1)/(C2) hold if and only if averaging is not beneficial. We state this formally in the following conjecture.

Conjecture 3.3. Consider gradient descent with stepsizes \(h = (h_0, \dots, h_{N-1}) > 0\). For any \(L, D > 0\), if the worst-case final iterate objective gap is not characterized by (C1), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}} f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr) - \inf f < \frac{LD^2}{4\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 2}.\]
Similarly, if the worst-case final iterate gradient norm is not characterized by (C2), then there exists nondegenerate \(\sigma\) such that
\[\max_{(x_0,f)\in\mathcal{F}_{L,D}} \Bigl\|\nabla f\Bigl(\sum_{j=0}^N \sigma_j x_j\Bigr)\Bigr\| < \frac{LD}{\sum_{i=0}^{N-1} h_i + 1}.\]
subject_classification: worst-case analysis of first-order methods in smooth convex optimization
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjecture is universal over stepsize sequences and smooth convex instances, so a complete counterexample search is not finite. However, PEP/SDP machinery can test many concrete stepsize sequences (fixed N) and can often produce candidate worst-case instances and certificates, making automated exploration for counterexamples plausible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate averaging weights \(\sigma\) can be parameterized and evaluated via solving the associated PEP SDP to obtain a worst-case value, yielding an automatic fitness signal for fixed \(N\) and \(h\). What is less direct is evolving proofs (or universally quantifying over \(h\)) rather than numerically optimizing \(\sigma\) for instances, so suitability is not high.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
